accurate.why: All claims match the bill text, and the contextual claims (IHRA definition adopted May 26, 2016, used by the State Department; F/M visas are student visas; INA citation) are factually correct.

no_hallucinations.why: No fabricated claims; calling F/M visas 'student visas' and the IHRA standard a 'working definition' used by State are accurate context, not hallucinations.

states_purpose.why: Summary clearly identifies the bill's purpose of revoking or denying student visas for those engaging in antisemitic conduct.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Summary notes the bill amends INA section 214 to add a new provision barring grant/maintenance of F/M status and mandating revocation.

exceptions_conditions.why: Summary correctly captures the two material conditions (prohibited conduct plus the foreign-policy-consequences determination under 237(a)(4)(C)(i)).

effective_dates.why: Summary notes the requirements apply to conduct occurring on or after the date of enactment, matching the bill.

major_provisions_covered.why: Summary captures both the Section 2 revocation/denial mandate and the Section 3 INA amendment, the bill's essential operative effects.

objective_tone.why: Summary is neutral and descriptive with no advocacy or editorializing.

coherent.why: Summary is well-organized, internally consistent, and reads clearly.

concise.why: Summary is appropriately concise without padding or repetition.

correct_entities.why: Secretary of State, Department of State, IHRA, and INA section 237(a)(4)(C)(i) are all correctly identified.

correct_figures.why: The only figure—the May 26, 2016 IHRA adoption date and the date-of-enactment trigger—are stated correctly.
